questions.description: According to the Property Registration Authority, By international comparison, Ireland has a very extensive and well-…
questions.text: According to the Property Registration Authority, By international comparison, Ireland has a very extensive and well-developed system of land registration. Since the foundation of the Land Registry in 1892, there has been a gradual, ongoing and continuous program of movement away from the older and limited system of recording Deeds (in the registry of Deeds) to the more modern, flexible and comprehensive 'title registration' system provided through the Land Registry. Considering the above statement, describe and discuss the system for recording property transactions in Ireland.

questions.description: Mary is 65 years old and for the past 30 years she has been employed as a housekeeper for Bridie at Bridie's house.
questions.text: Mary is 65 years old and for the past 30 years she has been employed as a housekeeper for Bridie at Bridie's house. When she first started working for Bridie, Mary received £35 a week, and this was never increased. Over the years, Bridie became increasingly dependent on Mary for things that went above and beyond the role of housekeeper. In fact, Mary had become Bridie's carer rather down her housekeeper. Despite the fact that there was no written agreement to this effect, Mary claims that Bridie had led her to believe that upon Bridie's death, Mary would be entitled to the house, or at the very least a right to remain in it. Mary stated that this was a mutual understanding between the parties. Bridie has now died and has left all of her property to the cats and dog's home. Mary seeks your advice on whether she has a claim to any interest in the house.

questions.description: The Residential Tenancies Act 2004 (as amended) when first enacted, contained a number of significant reforms for the…
questions.text: The Residential Tenancies Act 2004 (as amended) when first enacted, contained a number of significant reforms for the private sector in Ireland, in particular, in the area of security of tenure, and tenancy termination procedures. Consider whether the Residential Tenancies Act 2004 has succeeded in its aim of providing a clear legislative framework for tenancy obligations for landlords and tenants in Ireland.

questions.description: Outline and assess the Irish Courts' approach to the admissibility of extrinsic evidence in the construction of wills.
questions.text: Outline and assess the Irish Courts' approach to the admissibility of extrinsic evidence in the construction of wills.

questions.description: Tom and Gerry were in business together as solicitors and, in 2005,… Advise Gerry.
questions.text: Tom and Gerry were in business together as solicitors and, in 2005, decided to purchase a property in Galway to accommodate their practice. Each put €500,000 towards the purchase price of €1,000,000 and the property was conveyed "to Tom and Gerry". Relations have recently become strained between the two of them and Gerry now wishes to set up his own practice in Dublin. He wants to force the sale of the property or to make Tom pay him a rent if Tom continues to occupy the premises. Gerry has recently had a heart bypass operation and is concerned about whether his family would have a claim to the property if he happened to die before matters are resolved. Advise Gerry.

questions.description: Harry and Sally got married in 2012. Mortgage.
questions.text: Harry and Sally got married in 2012. They moved into their new home in Athlone, which was located on 100-acre farm in the countryside. Both the family home in the farm were in Harry's name. In 2015, Sally discovered that Harry was having an extra-marital affair with her best friend and so she moved back in with her parents in Cork. Harry distracted himself with the farm work and decided that an expansion of the business would be a good idea. He applied to take out a mortgage with the bank. When the bank asks if he is married, Harry replied, "My wife is dead to me". The bank took this literally and suggested that Harry would have to sign the statutory declaration to say that his wife was deceased in order to proceed with his application. Harry secured the mortgage. However, over time, the reality of the marriage breakdown hit Harry and he lost all interest in everything, including the farm. This resulted in him defaulting on his mortgage repayments from 2019. Sally recently became aware through Harry's sister that the bank now intends to sell the farm including the family home. Sally is furious and claims she never consented to this mortgage on the property. Advise Sally.

questions.description: When Louis discovered he had a terminal illness in January 2019, he… Advise Harvey.
questions.text: When Louis discovered he had a terminal illness in January 2019, he decided to draft a will. At the time, he was undergoing an aggressive form of treatment and so he didn't have the energy to go to his solicitor. He asked his husband, Harvey, to download a free will template from a website he had heard about. Louis invited their close neighbors, Tom and Jerry, over for dinner that evening with him and Harvey. Over a glass of wine after dinner, Louis began filling out a number of versions of his will. Once he was happy with the contents of the will of (€150,000 to his husband, Harvey, €20,000 to his niece, Donna and the remainder to the local animal rescue service), he asked Tom and Jerry if they could sign their names to the document. When Tom and Jerry left, Louis began tidying up. When disposing of the many versions of the will he had drafted and ripped up, he accidentally threw the proper version in the bin. The next morning, when Harvey realized Louis mistake, he cello-taped the will back together and put it in the drawer. In June 2020 comment Louis sadly passed away. Harvey recently brought the will to a local solicitors office where questions concerning the content and validity of the will have been raised. Advise Harvey.

questions.description: Write a note on [any two]{.underline} of the following.
questions.text: Write a note on [any two]{.underline} of the following. a) Bare licenses b) Commorientes c) Contractual licenses

questions.description: '[Adverse Possession's] controversy largely derives from a particular feature of its operation, whereby, to put it…
questions.text: '[Adverse Possession's] controversy largely derives from a particular feature of its operation, whereby, to put it somewhat crudely, a trespasser on someone's land may, if the trespass continues without disturbance for the statutory limitation period, become the owner of the land possessed. What appears extraordinary is that the legal system seems to be sanctioning and rewarding the committal of a civil wrong, without any question of compensation being paid to the dispossessed owner. This feature [has] assumed greater significance in recent times because of the emphasis on constitutional protection of property rights and human rights generally.' JCW Wylie, 'Adverse Possession: Still an Ailing Concept?' (2017) 58 Irish Jurist (n.s.) 1. Discuss. Your answer should consider whether adverse possession can be justified and, furthermore, if you would abolish, reform or leave untouched the Irish law of Adverse Possession.

questions.description: Outline and discuss the manner in which an easement may be acquired by prescription (i.e.
questions.text: Outline and discuss the manner in which an easement may be acquired by prescription (i.e. long use) under the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009, as amended.

questions.description: Set out the circumstances in which a business tenant will become entitled to a new tenancy under the Landlord and…
questions.text: Set out the circumstances in which a business tenant will become entitled to a new tenancy under the Landlord and Tenant (Amendment) Act 1980. Your answer should consider when a business tenant will become disentitled to such a new tenancy and whether such a tenant will be entitled to compensation.

Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b). Do not answer both (a) Outline and discuss, by reference to relevant case law, the circumstances in which indirect contributions to the purchase of a house by a non-owning spouse may lead to them acquiring an equity in the property (i.e. part ownership) based on these contributions. [Or]{.underline} (b) Discuss the strengthened protections that the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009, as amended, affords to mortgagors of 'house loan' mortgages (i.e. mortgagors of residential property as defined by the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009) who have defaulted on their loans.

questions.description: Set out (by reference to relevant case law and statutory provisions) the principles that the courts apply in the…
questions.text: Set out (by reference to relevant case law and statutory provisions) the principles that the courts apply in the construction of wills. Your answer should include a discussion of the circumstances when it has been deemed appropriate to allow extrinsic evidence to aid in this construction.

questions.description: A landlord has left property to a commercial tenant.
questions.text: A landlord has left property to a commercial tenant. The tenant has now breached a number of covenants in the lease including the covenant to pay rent. The landlord now seeks your advice. Set out in detail the forfeiture procedure and indicate how this procedure may compel the tenant to comply with the covenants in the lease, and failing that, how the lease can be terminated by forfeiture.

Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b). Do not answer both. (a) Outline and discuss with reference to relevant case law and statutory provisions how a joint tenancy may be severed (i.e. converted to a tenancy in common) both in law and in equity. [Or]{.underline} (b) In Irish law, when one joint tenant unlawfully kills another joint tenant and is convicted of this crime, what happens to the property of the deceased that was held as joint tenant during their life? Is this a fair solution in your view, or are there alternatives you might propose?

questions.description: Critically analyse the operation of section 117 of the Succession Act in 1965, which imposes a legal duty upon parents…
questions.text: Critically analyse the operation of section 117 of the Succession Act in 1965, which imposes a legal duty upon parents to make 'proper provision' for their children during the parent's lifetime or from their estate after death. Is this provision still fit for purpose in your view, or are there any amendments that should be made to it to better balance the rights of family members vis-a-vis the testator's freedom of testation?
